status: Evidence-backed working synthesis. Suitable for working-group review; not yet leadership-validated.

leadAnswer.statement: TAP should be framed as a lean, system-level portfolio and execution mechanism that attaches senior UN leadership decisions to distributed entity-level delivery capacity. It should not duplicate existing innovation hubs — its distinctive value is execution, not exchange.
points: UN innovation capacity is real but distributed — WFP, UNICEF, UNDP, UNHCR, ITU, Global Pulse, DPO and others each hold pieces of implementation capacity, but no single actor holds the whole.

System-level exchange exists, but system-level execution is weak. UNIN connects and curates; DTN coordinates technology actors; UN 2.0/Global Pulse provide capability and accelerator functions — none alone provides a durable, funded, senior-leadership-to-delivery mechanism.

The design gap is not another hub. It is a repeatable way to identify system-level priorities, attach funding and governance, assemble surge teams from existing entity capacity, and transition solutions to long-term owners.
The funding model is the "teeth". Without flexible pooled funding or committed entity contributions, TAP risks becoming just another coordination layer.

stats.label: From UN Talent aggregator / manual discovery

byRole.label: Embedded in other functions
byRole.note: Programme, digital, data, policy, partnership, operations

innovationJobs.takeaway: Only 28% of innovation-related roles are explicitly labelled 'innovation' — the other 72% are embedded across programme, digital, data, policy, partnership and operations functions. Capacity is real but distributed, which is the core evidence for TAP orchestrating existing entity capacity rather than building a new central hub.
innovationJobs.pending: Per-agency, role-family and geography distributions await re-running the innovation-jobs collector on the raw dataset (not yet in this repo).

meta.title: Innovation-jobs collection (augmenting the original 114-record scan)
meta.methodology: Real UN-system postings mentioning innovation, located via web research (June 2026) across Impactpool, ITU, UNDP and UNICEF careers listings. Every record carries a real source URL. Nothing is fabricated.
caveats: Discovery-grade evidence — verify each posting before citing as authoritative, consistent with the 'public secondary data / human review required' framing.
Post dates are left null where the source does not expose a reliable date; they populate automatically via the ReliefWeb collector.
This is NOT a complete 2020+ census. UN career portals expose only current openings, and the ReliefWeb / UN Talent bulk endpoints are gated; coverage skews to recent/current roles.
meta.fullPull: For the complete 2020-onwards UN innovation-jobs pull, register a free ReliefWeb appname at https://apidoc.reliefweb.int/parameters#appname, set RELIEFWEB_APPNAME in .env, and run `npm run collect:jobs` — it adds dated, archived postings (closed roles included) to this file automatically.

rq.question: Who are the key actors and stakeholders in the UN innovation ecosystem?
rq.answer: Map the ecosystem by function, not by logo: entity-level implementation capacity (WFP, UNICEF, UNDP, UNHCR, ITU, DPO and other entity innovation/digital/data teams); system-level exchange and knowledge (UNIN, UN 2.0 communities of practice, capacity-building actors); coordination and decision channels (DTN, HLCM/CEB mechanisms, the UN80 Steering Committee while active); flexible delivery enablers (Global Pulse, UNICC, Digital ID/Data Commons coalitions, pooled funds); and adoption owners — programme, country, or shared-service owners who can commit to transition and reuse.

rq.question: How is innovation currently delivered across entity and cross-entity levels?
rq.answer: Innovation is delivered through a mixed architecture — entity accelerators and labs, digital/data teams, programme-led pilots, funds, communities of practice, and ad hoc inter-agency coalitions. No single UN model dominates, which supports TAP as an orchestrator and execution-enabler rather than a centralized replacement.
evidence: Johanna interview (models similar but not codified)

rq.question: What processes, roles, and barriers characterize the ecosystem?
rq.answer: The process moves idea/demand signal → pilot/prototype → internal validation → attempted scale. Support breaks at ownership, technical delivery, flexible funding, procurement/legal, cross-entity resource sharing, and adoption/transition. Funding is the named problem but is often a proxy for missing execution resources.

rq.question: Where is the ecosystem strongest, and where are the gaps?
rq.answer: The ecosystem is strongest at entity-level innovation and distributed technical/programmatic expertise. The critical gap is system-level implementation capacity: a mechanism that lets senior leadership decide priorities and then mobilize entity-level capacity for system-level use cases.

rq.question: How do innovations travel through hubs, and where does support break?
rq.answer: Grassroots ideas and entity innovations move through WFP, Global Pulse, UNICEF, UNIN and other channels, but they often fail to cross the chasm into multi-entity adoption. Support breaks when there is no mandate, no common owner, no pooled funding, no technical "doing" team, or no sustainable service-provider/cost-recovery route.
evidence: Talea interview (Global Pulse cohorts, Data Commons)

rq.question: What operating models exist, and what do they teach TAP?
rq.answer: Learn from at least five models: WFP Innovation Accelerator (structured path to scale with funding, mentorship, hands-on support and operational access); UNICEF Venture Fund (staged non-dilutive funding, open-source requirement, real-time data, growth-stage funding); Global Pulse accelerator (system-wide cohort/support and flexible innovation capacity); Digital ID/Data Commons (small technical ownership plus a cross-entity leadership mandate); and CRAF'd/MPTF (pooled financing, steering committee, secretariat, administrative agent, results reporting).

rq.question: What external accelerator best practices should be adapted?
rq.answer: Adapt the substantive practices, not cosmetic accelerator rituals: stage gates and stop/go decisions; fast sprints for uncertainty reduction; adoption-owner checks before scale funding; structured technical support and a partner bench; portfolio-level measurement rather than idea-count; and a clear transition route into operations, cost recovery, or service-provider ownership.

rq.question: What criteria define TAP-worthy digital/technology innovation?
rq.answer: A TAP-worthy use case clears four filters: new or newly adapted to context (not routine ICT maintenance); technology-enabled (digital, data, AI, platform, emerging technology, or shared technology component); system-wide potential (value crosses entities, countries, or shared functions); and a real adoption pathway (named problem/adoption owner, transition route, and measurable benefit). Explicit exclusions: generic helpdesk work, routine data entry, ordinary communications, and one-entity IT maintenance.

rq.question: What pain points block delivery and scale?
rq.answer: The main blockers are fragmentation, funding constraints, policy/legal/governance constraints, limited technical capacity, weak adoption ownership, earmarked funding, time scarcity, and culture/leadership gaps. The TAP Business Needs Survey supports these categories, but the raw survey still needs validation before its figures are treated as final.
evidence: TAP survey slide in working draft

rq.question: What support is needed at different maturity levels?
rq.answer: Support changes by maturity — Discovery/demand: surveys, communities, accelerator pipelines, country/team pain points, and horizon scanning. Seed/pilot: sprint funding, technical assessment, project management, and data/security/legal checks. Implementation: assemble a surge task force, secure owner commitments, and de-block procurement/legal/governance. Scale: fund adoption and interoperability work and connect service providers and country/entity adopters. Transition: hand over to a shared service provider, implementing entity, cost-recovery mechanism, or pooled-fund window.

rq.question: What should inform demand signals, prioritization, and metrics?
rq.answer: Demand should not rely only on a Microsoft Forms-style survey. Triangulate: business-needs survey results, existing accelerator/case pipelines, official innovation/digital job postings and capability signals, pooled-funding and donor-priority signals, CIO/DTN/UNIN/UN 2.0 community inputs, and use-case progress data from Data Commons, Beneficiary ID, Translation, and Expertise-on-Demand. Prioritize by system value, urgency, adoption readiness, reuse potential, technical feasibility, risk, and availability of owners.
evidence: Talea interview (WFP demand signal structurally smarter)

rq.question: Who is TAP's client?
rq.answer: TAP's client is the accountable problem/adoption owner, not simply the innovator. Innovators are partners. The client must be able to commit to adoption, funding or cost recovery, a legal/policy path, and transition. For system-level assets, this may be a coalition or shared-service owner.

rq.question: What operating, governance, and funding model should TAP use?
rq.answer: Use a hybrid model: a lean, execution-focused TAP Coordination Unit (prepares the portfolio, tracks delivery, supports fundraising, maintains evidence); time-bound Surge Task Forces built around use cases from entity experts, technical capacity, project management and vendors/partners; the UN Innovation Ecosystem as the source of knowledge, demand, solutions, owners and experts; a Steering Committee for quarterly approval of portfolio, funding envelope, stage decisions and escalation; and pooled funding (a flexible fund or joint programme with clear safeguards) rather than slow, donor-driven case-by-case fundraising as the default. Governance must separate problem selection, technical validation, funding allocation, delivery oversight, and adoption commitment.

rq.question: What investment posture and KPIs should TAP use?
rq.answer: Adopt a catalytic, stage-based public-value venture posture: small, fast, flexible seed/sprint tickets for learning and feasibility; larger targeted scale-up funding after evidence and owner commitment; and a sustainable transition route through a service provider, cost recovery, pooled fund, or entity handover. KPIs should track portfolio flow and adoption rather than idea volume — seed and scale-up counts, time from demand signal to funded setup, number of entities contributing to surge task forces, named adoption owners and completed transitions, reuse across entities/country teams, value delivered vs. business case, and integrity/risk indicators (cybersecurity, data protection, ethics, procurement/legal readiness).

rq.question: What pathway and risks should guide TAP setup?
rq.answer: Move from UN80 temporary governance into a standing joint programme or MPTF-style mechanism: mandate and sponsor route → demand and portfolio thesis → first proposals and owner commitments → delivery through surge teams and pooled capacity → transition to sustainable owners → institutionalize as a standing UN 2.0 acceleration capability. Key risks: coordination-only failure (no technical team, authority, or money); duplication with WFP, UNICEF, Global Pulse, UNIN, DTN or UNICC; funding capture by donor preferences; ownership failure (no adoption owner or transition route); evidence weakness (WIP survey/deck figures cited as final without raw validation); unclear post-UN80 governance; and security/legal risk on AI/data/shared-platform use cases that need early risk gating.

strongNow: Direction of travel: TAP as a lean system-level accelerator/joint programme — anchored in the UN80 report and working draft.
Distributed capacity — supported by the innovation-jobs dataset, interviews, and the UNGP report.
System-level execution gap — strongly supported by the Johanna and Talea interviews.
Need for pooled funding and governance — supported by Talea, CRAF'd/MPTF, and the working-draft funding slides.
useWithCaveats: TAP Business Needs Survey percentages — cite as internal WIP until the raw survey and methodology are checked.
Gartner benchmark — cite only if the underlying source/table can be shown.
UN Talent aggregator roles — use for discovery, not authoritative evidence.
UN 2.0 Assessment data — useful, but confirm the raw assessment and sharing status.

agents.name: Source Watcher
agents.does: Watches the TAP folder, UN80 docs, MPTF Gateway, IATI, WFP, UNICEF, Global Pulse, OECD OPSI, and CEB/HLCM/DTN pages. Emits a changed-source registry with URL/path, date, owner, access status, and evidence type.

agents.name: Document Extractor
agents.does: Converts PPTX/PDF/DOCX/XLSX/HTML to text; emits extracts, metadata, keyword snippets, and duplicate detection.

agents.name: Innovation Jobs
agents.does: Re-runs and extends the innovation-jobs collector across WHO/FAO/Taleo, Inspira, UNFPA, UNOPS, and UNICC; emits role distributions by agency, source type, role family, and geography.

agents.name: Funding & Governance
agents.does: Pulls MPTF Gateway fund/project/contribution data and IATI activities; tags pooled funds by governance model, contributors, administrative agent, participating organizations, and disbursement pattern.

agents.name: Case Study
agents.does: Tracks WFP, UNICEF, Global Pulse, CRAF'd, Digital ID/Data Commons, NHS, and OECD OPSI cases; extracts stage gates, support package, funding ticket size, adoption owner, scale metrics, and transition model.

agents.name: Demand Signal
agents.does: Compares the TAP survey, accelerator pipelines, UNIN/UN 2.0 community signals, jobs data, and funding flows; emits recurring demand themes and survey-only vs. externally-validated.

agents.name: Claim Auditor
agents.does: Maintains the claim ledger: claim text, source IDs, evidence type, confidence, validation status, stale date, deck slide, and next check; emits safe-to-cite / cite-with-caveat / do-not-cite-yet.

agents.name: Synthesis / Deck
agents.does: Produces slide-ready bullets and a weekly research delta; never invents final claims — only promotes from needs_review to working_confident when the Claim Auditor has enough evidence.

cadence.task: Synthesis agent prepares deck deltas and interview-question updates.
Human review with Rahul / working team; promote only reviewed claims.

sources.name: MPTF Gateway
sources.useFor: Pooled fund structures, contributors, participating organizations, project financials, payment vouchers, and CRAF'd governance.

sources.name: Complex Risk Analytics Fund (CRAF'd)
sources.useFor: Concrete pooled-fund benchmark for data/digital public goods: steering committee, secretariat, administrative agent, and funding windows.

sources.name: IATI API Gateway
sources.useFor: Development/humanitarian activity data, donor-recipient flows, sectors, locations, implementing orgs, and duplication/coordination evidence.

sources.name: WFP Innovation Accelerator
sources.useFor: Path-to-scale model, funding/mentorship/hands-on support, and operational access.

sources.name: UNICEF Venture Fund
sources.useFor: Staged non-dilutive funding, open-source requirements, real-time data requirement, and growth funding model.

sources.name: UN Global Pulse
sources.useFor: SG innovation-lab positioning, Global Pulse accelerator/hubs, and flexible innovation capacity.

sources.name: OECD OPSI
sources.useFor: Public-sector innovation benchmark cases and external case-study coding.

nextActions: Validate the raw TAP Business Needs Survey before using percentages as final evidence.
Turn the ecosystem map into a database with actor type, level, decision rights, implementation capacity, funding model, and evidence source.
Update the interview guide for WFP around demand-signal design, private-sector partnership, and path-to-scale governance.
Interview Max / Digital ID as a system-level delivery case with a technical owner plus institutional mandate.
Interview DPO / a smaller entity to avoid over-designing for mature agencies only.
Run the secondary-data agent loop weekly and maintain a claim ledger before every deck update.
